coredata.dc:title: Performance characterization of lithium-ion battery cells within restricted operating range using an extended ragone plot

coredata.dc:creator: Wiegelmann, Sven
Bensmann, Astrid
Hanke-Rauschenbach, Richard
coredata.dc:description:
Lithium-ion battery systems play a crucial role in applications ranging from electric vehicles to grid storage, but their performance can vary significantly under different operating conditions. While extensive research has explored the non-linear relationship of the battery’s key performance metrics – energy and power – across the manufacturers’ permissible limits, the impacts of operating in a restricted range have not yet been sufficiently investigated. Restricting the available operating range, particularly the upper voltage limit, may be advantageous or even necessary for specific applications with harsh environmental conditions, potentially enhancing safety, efficiency, compatibility, and lifespan without substantially compromising performance. In order to investigate this trade-off, the power-based performance of three battery cells with different formats and chemistries is experimentally characterized and analyzed using an extended Ragone plot. To reduce experimental effort, we demonstrate a reconstruction-based approach to recalculate the Ragone plot for arbitrary voltage initialization limits by trimming the full-range dataset based on the preceding charge termination conditions. In the practically relevant range, deviations between the measured and reconstructed Ragone curves remain within
≤
3
% , validated by an electrical and thermal assessment. By superimposing upper and lower operating limits, the extended Ragone plot enables an evaluation of battery performance under a restricted range without additional cell characterization measurements. Our findings thus provide a practical and efficient method for engineers and researchers, ideally supporting the decision-making in selecting, designing, or managing battery systems for application-specific energy storage solutions.

head.ce:title: Performance characterization of lithium-ion battery cells within restricted operating range using an extended ragone plot

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Lithium-ion battery systems play a crucial role in applications ranging from electric vehicles to grid storage, but their performance can vary significantly under different operating conditions. While extensive research has explored the non-linear relationship of the battery’s key performance metrics – energy and power – across the manufacturers’ permissible limits, the impacts of operating in a restricted range have not yet been sufficiently investigated. Restricting the available operating range, particularly the upper voltage limit, may be advantageous or even necessary for specific applications with harsh environmental conditions, potentially enhancing safety, efficiency, compatibility, and lifespan without substantially compromising performance. In order to investigate this trade-off, the power-based performance of three battery cells with different formats and chemistries is experimentally characterized and analyzed using an extended Ragone plot. To reduce experimental effort, we demonstrate a reconstruction-based approach to recalculate the Ragone plot for arbitrary voltage initialization limits by trimming the full-range dataset based on the preceding charge termination conditions. In the practically relevant range, deviations between the measured and reconstructed Ragone curves remain within
≤
3
% , validated by an electrical and thermal assessment. By superimposing upper and lower operating limits, the extended Ragone plot enables an evaluation of battery performance under a restricted range without additional cell characterization measurements. Our findings thus provide a practical and efficient method for engineers and researchers, ideally supporting the decision-making in selecting, designing, or managing battery systems for application-specific energy storage solutions.

ce:section.ce:para:
The present work focuses on the power-based characterization of different lithium-ion batteries within a restricted operating range and investigates the influence on their key performance indicators, i.e., energy ( E ) and power ( P ), in a Ragone plot. To ensure safe operation and protect their battery from damage, cell manufacturers specify permissible limits for all relevant electrical and thermal operating variables, including voltage ( U ), current ( I ), or temperature ( ϑ ). The cell voltage
U
cell
, representing the electrical potential variable of this energy storage device, significantly influences the performance characteristics of the battery. Its full operating range is defined by a minimum (
U
cell , min
) and maximum (
U
cell , max
) voltage limit to prevent overdischarge [1,2] and overcharge [3,4], which could lead to safety issues by material decomposition [5], lithium plating [6] or internal short circuits [5,7] and finally trigger exothermic side reactions, also known as thermal runaway [3,8]. The specific values of both limits depend on the battery’s material chemistry and internal structure, varying by cell type.

Within this safe operating range
[
U
cell , min
,
U
cell , max
]
, the nominal capacity (
Q
cell , n
) or the nominal energy (
E
cell , n
) of the battery can be discharged under nominal conditions. Especially in an application context, instead of voltage, the available operating range of a cell is frequently normalized to the interval
[ 0 , 1 ]
, either as a state of charge (
SoC
) with
Q
cell , n
or as a state of energy (
SoE
) with
E
cell , n
. Additionally, depth of discharge (
DoD
) is often used to describe the cell’s cyclization range, typically referring to a fully charged (
SoC
cell
= 100
% ) or an average state within this range. Depending on the literature, particularly application-oriented works, these state descriptors SoC, SoE, and DoD are occasionally used to specify the operating range of the battery cell instead of the cell voltage
U
cell
.

However, the battery’s voltage behavior is critical from a safety perspective. While the minimum and maximum voltage limits protect against irreversible damage, further restricting the available operating range for specific applications or harsh environmental conditions may be advantageous or even necessary. Such restrictions can help lower the risk of unsafe conditions, enhance the battery’s efficiency and system compatibility, and extend its lifespan.

Numerous empirical studies at the cell level have investigated the effects of increasing the lower voltage limit and decreasing the upper voltage limit on different aspects of degradation. For instance, float current analyses with commercial NMC
|
C-, LFP
|
C-, and NCA
|
C-based battery cells have shown that growth of the solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) passivation layer dominates calendar aging towards both their voltage limits [9]. Cyclic aging has also been extensively studied, particularly in NMC
|
C cells, where lithium inventory loss, driven primarily by SEI growth, is strongly influenced by higher DoD operating ranges [10,11], and their charge-transfer resistances increased significantly at higher cell voltage [12]. In contrast, LFP
|
C cells exhibit pronounced capacity fade at high voltages, regardless of the DoD range [13], while NCA
|
C cells, especially under low temperatures, reveal accelerated aging when expanding their operating range towards one or both voltage limits [14]. Various modeling approaches, e.g., based on degradation maps [15], Wöhler curves [16] or degradation modes [17] have already attempted to generalize these findings, showing a disproportionate increase in degradation rate, with most cell chemistries degrading faster at high SoC, while some also degrade faster at low SoC. Despite gaps in mechanistic understanding and the need to harmonize findings across these studies, an evident correlation between the operating range restriction and a significant deceleration in capacity fade and resistance increase is found, resulting in improved cycling stability for all battery cells. Consequently, careful DoD management is essential for extending cycle life and thus reducing operating costs [18,19].

Efficiency considerations further support the case for restricting the operating range. Studies have found a rapid decline in energy efficiency with decreasing DoD range [19] and a drop in Coulombic efficiency when lowering the discharge cut-off voltage, especially in low-temperature environments [14,20]. These effects are often linked to the characteristic tub-shaped curve of the battery’s internal resistance as a function of SoC, where resistance increases significantly near one or both operating limits and rises sharply in overcharge conditions [cf. 21,22].

From a system design and integration perspective, restricting the operating range may also be necessary to ensure compatibility between battery cells and other system components. Suppose the battery cell’s full-range performance characteristics do not align with the available operating range of the power electronics or other interconnected devices. In that case, engineers can facilitate system integration by reducing the cell’s usable operating range during the design process, ensuring that all system requirements are efficiently met [cf. 23–25].

Operational management strategies, such as derating and fast charging protocols, are crucial for dynamically controlling and adjusting battery operating parameters to enhance safety and extend system lifespan. Exemplary derating strategies include SoC-based power derating, known as “limp home” mode in electric vehicles [26], to ensure battery operation within safe limits even under stress, or degradation-aware current [27] and cut-off voltage derating [28], likewise facilitating thermal management. Fast charge protocols focus on adapting the maximum cut-off voltage to minimize temperature rise and degradation [29,30], e.g., by indirect control of the negative electrode polarization [31]. Moreover, operating range restrictions are essential for optimizing resource allocation in multi-use battery storage systems, such as reserving energy and power bands for specific functions like peak shaving or frequency containment reserve [32].

Despite the clear benefits of restricting the operating range for safety, efficiency, compatibility, and lifespan, it would inevitably reduce the achievable capacity and energy of the battery, potentially leading to more extensive system designs with increased weight, volume, and costs. It is, therefore, crucial to find a suitable trade-off for adapting the usable operating range and balancing these aspects optimally in order to meet the performance requirements of each specific energy storage application.

When it comes to the decision-making of such trade-offs, the Ragone plot, named after the work of Ragone [33], serves as an efficient concept for evaluation, comparison, and visualization of different energy storage technologies in terms of their key performance metrics, E and P . It represents the characteristic non-linear relationship between these metrics by an enveloping curve based on E
( P )
coordinates or normalized to the corresponding volume- or weight-specific values of the specific storage gained from multiple constant power measurements [34]. In the field of batteries, Ragone plots are widely used for energy storage selection and sizing, particularly including applications like vehicle and truck powertrains, either full-electric [35,36] or in a hybrid topology for coupling different battery types [37,38] or batteries with supercapacitors [36,39] and fuel cells [40], but also stationary power grid storage systems for buffering fluctuations in renewable energies [41]. However, most of the Ragone plots known to the authors only reflect the battery performance concerning the full operating range specified by the manufacturer’s permissible limits. Only few studies have investigated the influence of a restricted operating range on the battery’s energy-power characteristics.

For instance, a study by Krieger and Arnold [42] analyzed the effects of “undercharge” and “underdischarge” by adjusting the specific cut-off voltages for distributed set powers in either charge or discharge directions. They adapted the theoretical model of Christen and Karlen [43], allowing for mathematical calculation of the Ragone envelope for various energy storage devices, and experimentally validated it across the full operating range. The observed loss in available capacity Δ Q
( P )
was attributed to a resistance-induced voltage offset causing earlier termination. Additionally, LeBel et al. [24] presented a battery pack sizing method for electric motorcycle applications, where they manually restricted the battery’s available operating range by setting a higher discharge voltage limit
U
cell , min
, corresponding to
SoC
cell , min
= 20
% , to enhance safety and compatibility. However, their performance metrics were derived from data sheet-based discharge curves at constant current (CC), which, according to the experimental findings of Zhang et al. [44], can deviate by up to 25
% versus constant power (CP) at the same operating point.

Further studies by Sarpal et al. [45] and Wiegelmann et al. [23] systematically quantified the impact of multiple operating conditions at different cells, including variations of termination limits like
U
cell , min
,
I
cell , max
dis
, and
ϑ
cell , max
dis
, on the discharge performance of different batteries through CP measurements. This led to the development of the extended Ragone plot (ERP) in our previous work [23], allowing for re-evaluation of the battery’s Ragone envelope by trimming the full-range dataset with one or multiple combined limits. As the ERP can incorporate both directly measurable and calculable limits, such as
SoC
cell , min
, it has proved well suited for conceptual decision-making in a system design method.

Although several battery cells have been investigated under a variation of many operating conditions causing termination of the respective discharge or charge measurements, research on the variation of initialization conditions remains sparse. Only a few investigations have varied the initial temperatures
ϑ
cell , init
by setting different
ϑ
amb
during characterization, such as Catenaro et al. [35,49], to derive an enhanced Ragone plot based on CC measurements with three cells for energy storage selection and sizing, or Verbrugge and Ying [50] to parameterize a theoretical battery model as a function of temperature. Sarpal et al. were the only ones to investigate the influence of restricting the operating range by varying the initial state-of-charge
SoC
cell , init
for part-load applications, where, for example, it is impracticable to recharge the cells completely before every energy request. While their study experimentally characterized the influence of these initialization conditions on the particular charge or discharge performance, it was primarily for illustrative purposes and only superficially analyzed. To date, we are not aware of any comprehensive work that examines the performance characteristics of a battery cell in a restricted operating range where its lower and upper limits are adjusted concurrently.

This paper aims to fill this gap by experimentally characterizing the power-based performance of lithium-ion battery cells within a restricted operating range, specifically focusing on a variation of the upper voltage limit first. While the sole adjustment of different termination limits, such as the discharge cut-off voltage, has been investigated in our previous publication, this study concentrates on the variation of the initialization condition by investigating the influence of the end-of-charge voltage on the subsequent discharge performance. An ERP is employed to visualize and harmonize the individual measurement results of this performance characterization. To reduce the experimental effort, we demonstrate a reconstruction method to recalculate the Ragone envelope under variation of the upper voltage limit from the full-range measurement. Using a superposition of the upper and lower voltage limits, performance metrics for the restricted operating range are finally derived using this reconstruction-based approach. Our approach seeks to enable comprehensive battery performance analysis without the need to characterize various operating limit combinations individually, providing a practical and efficient method for engineers and researchers.

The article is structured as follows: Section 2 presents the specifications of the selected battery cells and the test equipment used for performance characterization. Following the measurement-based approach, Section 3 presents the test procedure before the experimental results of characterizing the battery cells’ performance under varying upper voltage limits are analyzed and discussed using an ERP. Section 4 proposes the reconstruction-based approach to trim the full-range measurement data from the previous section to match the same voltage limits and enable the extraction of the cell’s performance characteristics. After clarifying the procedure, the results of this approach are comparatively analyzed with those of the corresponding measurements for validation, and probable causes for deviations are discussed in detail for all battery cells. In Section 5, the superposition of simultaneous range restrictions by altered termination and initialization limits calculated from reconstruction is examined in the ERP using the operating voltage as an example. Finally, Section 6 summarizes the main results, highlights the limitations of the proposed approach, and provides an outlook for future research.
This section presents the specifications of the battery cells and test equipment examined to carry out the performance characterization.

For this study, three types of lithium-ion battery cells were selected. Table 1 lists their relevant datasheet specifications. In order to discuss different influencing factors in the presented analysis, cells with different types, formats, and cell chemistries were chosen. Thus, the physical properties, such as available current, temperature, and especially voltage ranges, are cell-specific.

Prismatic cell #1, with the largest size and thus current capacity, is based on lithium titanium oxide (LTO) as negative electrode material. Screening its discharge curves from the datasheet, we suspect the positive electrode is a two-phase solid mixture of lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC) and lithium manganese oxide (LMO). Not uncommon due to the stable spinel structure of LTO and its elevated half-cell potential curve [51,52], this cell offers the lowest internal resistance, totaling 1
m Ω
but also the lowest permissible cell voltage limits of only 1.5 and 2.7
V
. However, the material’s enhanced stability enables symmetrical electrical and thermal operating ranges in the charging and discharging direction with maximum continuous currents of 4.4
C
and a maximum temperature of 55
∘
C
.

Cell #2 is an elongated pouch cell with a geometric aspect ratio of
>
3.3 . From the datasheet, it is specified with a positive electrode of lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) and a carbon-based negative electrode, usually made of graphite (C), which corresponds to the highest overall voltage range up to 4.2
V
, the typical physical limit to protect the LCO material against structural instability [53,54]. In the discharge direction, very high continuous current rates of 15
C
and a maximum temperature of 60
∘
C
are permitted, while its charge direction is considerably limited.

Cell #3, on the other hand, is a cylindrical nano-lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cell in 26,650 format. With 2.6
Ah
, it offers the smallest nominal charge capacity of the analyzed battery cells, but it is approved for the highest current rates at 20
C
permanently and even 48
C
for pulses. Like the LCO-based pouch cell, the current and temperature ranges of cell #3 are more limited in the charge direction, but despite its lower cell capacity, it offers similar discharge characteristics overall. Typical of an LFP-based battery cell are its relatively moderate cell voltage of 3.3
V
and permissible voltage range width of 1.1
V
.

A Scienlab SL60/300/18BT2C test system was used to experimentally test the performance of the selected battery cells. It offers a 6
V
voltage range for single-cell cyclization measurements and a bidirectional current range of ± 300
A
, allowing for high power charge and discharge. Voltage is measured by an analog 4-wire sensor with an accuracy of ± 0.05
% of the reading ± 1
mV
, while the accuracy of the current measurement is ± 0.05
% of the reading ± 60
mA
. In order to establish consistent ambient conditions for each test, the electrical cyclization is conducted under homogeneous temperature
ϑ
amb
= 25
∘
C
and free convection inside a BINDER type MK 720 temperature chamber. The temperature is measured using a Pt100 4-wire measurement with an accuracy of ± 1
K
in the interval − 50
∘
C
to 130
∘
C
. The error due to the measurement uncertainties is estimated to be largely negligible in the relevant value range of the battery cell’s measurement signals. For verification, a measurement uncertainty analysis was conducted based on the experimental results of each cell, which are presented in the following section (see Supplementary data, Section S.1).

This section introduces the measurement-based approach for characterizing battery cell performance. First, the measurement procedure is explained, followed by the analysis and discussion of the measurement results for the selected battery cells.

To visualize and compare their performance metrics in an extended Ragone plot, we performed characterization measurements on these three battery cell types with reduced charge cut-off voltages. At least two samples of each cell type were tested in parallel to ensure the reproducibility of the measurement results, although the results shown are from one reference cell only.

Subsequently, the ambient conditions of the temperature chamber are standardized to a temperature of

= 25

C
(Climate) before the electrical cyclization of the battery cells is initiated, as represented by the two nested loops for iterating over

in the bottom section of the flow chart.

An electric cycle consists of a nominal charge (Source (chg)), a subsequent discharge step (Source (dis)), and two resting phases in between (Wait). According to the manufacturer’s specifications, each cell is charged with nominal constant current (CC)
I
cell , n
chg
to an upper voltage
U
cell , cut
chg
, which is set by iterating
i
U
, followed by a constant voltage (CV) phase at this end-of-charge voltage until the current drops to a specific cut-off current
I
cell , cut
chg
. The discharge steps, in contrast, are performed at varying constant power (CP), taking previously calculated
P
cell
dis
[
i
P
]
from Eq. (3) as the set value. After each charge and discharge, a 15-min resting phase lets the polarized cells briefly relax toward their electrical–thermal equilibrium state.

In contrast to the standard protocol for experimentally determining a Ragone plot [45], the novel aspect of this procedure is embedding the corresponding inner loop into an additional outer one for reducing the upper voltage limit in the CV phase. This modification allows cell characterization within a restricted voltage range, depending on variations of
U
cell , max
, by incomplete recharge. Further characterization measurements could also be derived under different operating conditions, such as varying ambient temperature, by simply adjusting the outer loop in the same test procedure.

Briefly summarizing the measurement results of the three battery cells, an analysis of their ERPs reveals considerable energy deficits associated with the charge cut-off voltage reduction. The specific cell chemistry is identified as the dominant influencing factor on their respective performance characteristics. Reducing the upper voltage may benefit system design or operational management, particularly if harmful operating ranges are avoided without substantially impacting performance, especially for battery cells with low gradients of relative energy decrease δ
E
cell , unav
dis
/
δ
U
cell , cut
chg
. Our analysis shows that this appears suitable for the LFP- and LTO-based cells. However, for the LCO cell, a linear decrease in energy is anticipated by restricting the end-of-charge voltage.

Due to high experimental effort, characterizing each cell at multiple cut-off voltage levels through individual measurements is costly and time-consuming. We therefore want to examine whether and under which assumptions the required performance characteristics of a battery cell can be estimated based only on the full-range measurements of the base case. Therefore, we propose a reconstruction-based approach, explained below. This approach is applied to the measurement data from the previous section for validation, while probable causes for deviations are discussed in detail for the selected battery cells.

The fundamental concept of recalculating a battery cell’s ERP for a variation of specific operating limits is analogous to our previous publication [23]. Utilizing a dataset collected within the full permissible operating range during CP discharge, the cell’s performance characteristics can be adjusted by trimming at various measurable limits, such as
U
cell , min
,
I
cell , max
dis
, or
ϑ
cell , max
dis
, as well as calculated limits, such as
SoC
cell , min
, each forcing termination. A critical assumption of this trimming procedure is that the temporal dynamics of the base case measurements are independent of the termination condition. This assumption holds for various termination limits, such as
U
cell , cut
dis
=
U
cell , min
.

However, modifying limits associated with the begin-of-charge or -discharge can significantly affect the temporal dynamics of the cell’s operation. As analyzed in Section 2, the initial discharge voltage
U
cell , init
dis
from an electrical perspective and the initial temperature
ϑ
cell , init
dis
from a thermal perspective are the relevant initialization conditions for each discharge. Since this study primarily focuses on the influence of electrical operating range restrictions on cell performance, only the initial discharge voltage shall be considered as the target value for the trimming process. 2
2 Although the initial discharge temperature is maintained at approximately

cell , init
dis

cell
(
ϑ
cell
)
and correspondingly on the trim. However, especially for cell #1, characterized by a gradual temperature increase and moderate overall heating during discharge even at maximum power, we posit that the initial temperature has little influence on the ERP recalculated by trimming. This assumption will be reassessed in Section 4.3 for all battery cells.

Corresponding pOCV measurements for cells #2 and #3 are available in the Supplementary data, Section S.2.2.

Ultimately, the reconstruction procedure for characterizing cell performance with a reduction of the upper voltage limit can be summarized as follows: Starting with the full-range data used to generate a Ragone plot, the cut-off conditions from the preceding charge process must be matched to determine the initialization condition of the subsequent discharge accurately. Since the nominal CCCV charge terminates at
I
cell , cut
chg
, the characteristic
pOCV
chg
curve recorded with this current is assumed to represent the cut-off conditions for subsequent discharges. It allows matching the cut-off state
SoC
cell , cut
for any given upper voltage
U
cell , cut
chg
, which is why we will refer to it as “SoC matching” in the following. Finally, to account for the polarization-dependent initial discharge voltages, the upper limit of the desired voltage range is reconstructed as
U
cell , init
dis
= f (
SoC
cell , cut
) , and the base case data is trimmed accordingly along this cut-off SoC for each CP discharge.

Since this effect only occurs at low
U
cell , cut
chg
, it can be plausibilized by analyzing the temperature signals in Fig. 8(a2)–(c2) for
i
P
= 9 . According to the measurement procedure outlined in Fig. 1, all CP discharges start at an approximately equal initial temperature

cell , init

=

. In contrast, during reconstruction, the initial cell temperature is determined by the temperature

= f
(

cell , cut
)
at the trimming point from the base case measurement
(
i
U
= 1 )
, incorporating the already accumulated thermal energy. According to previous research [62,63], the temperature dependence of a lithium-ion battery cell’s internal resistance
R
i , cell
= f
(
ϑ
cell
)
generally follows an Arrhenius law [cf. 64]. A higher cell temperature thus lowers the internal resistance, thereby reducing polarization and increasing the voltage
U
cell
in the direction of discharge. Under the same operating range restriction, a colder cell reaches the termination at
U
cell , min
earlier during the measurement, providing slightly less energy compared to the corresponding reconstruction of the warmer base case. This temperature effect becomes more influential when (i) the set power causes notable heating of the battery cell and (ii)
U
cell , cut
chg
is significantly reduced, i.e., the trim occurs later in the base case. Based on the reference discharge at
i
P
= 9 , this temperature effect can be observed particularly well for cell #2 in Fig. 8(b).

Apart from this, additional discharges at notably lower and higher power values are evaluated in the Supplementary data, Section S.3.1. As expected from the ERPs in Fig. 7(a1)–(c1), the temperature effect on the energy deviations δ
E
cell
dis
between measurement and reconstruction becomes evident at very high continuous power for all cells (see Fig. S.3.2). In contrast to cell #1, cells #2 and #3 heat up significantly, affecting their cell voltages and extractable discharge energies, as discussed earlier. Even for cell #1, which experiences only moderate temperature rise, the measurement results provide less energy than predicted by the reconstruction.

At low discharge power, the temperature increase is minimal across all cells, suggesting that the temperature effect on ERP deviations can be regarded as negligible. However, deviations between measurement and reconstruction occur, especially for cell #3 (see Fig. S.3.1). Given the approximately equal initial temperature conditions, these deviations cannot be attributed to the temperature effect but rather to uncertainties in the SoC matching. An additional investigation is included in the Supplementary data, Section S.3.2, analyzing the effect of intentionally varying the
pOCV
references for SoC matching within the reconstruction-based approach.

This analysis emphasizes that only the
pOCV
chg
curve provides an accurate reference for calculating the initialization conditions of a subsequent discharge process by reconstruction. SoC matching based on the
pOCV
avg
curve, e.g., from datasheets, or any reference curve measured at low current in the discharge direction, such as
pOCV
dis
, would inevitably overestimate the available cell energy at the same set power due to their slightly lower reference voltages compared to the
pOCV
chg
curve and should consequently be avoided. In general, the accuracy of SoC matching is highly sensitive to the underlying cell voltage data, especially for battery cells with a flat voltage plateau and pronounced electrical polarization behavior, e.g., due to high internal resistance (see Table 1) or voltage hysteresis, as observed with cell #3. Reliable trimming is thus only recommended in marginal parts of the operating voltage curve where the cell contains a low incremental capacity (IC) defined as
d

/

cell
, i.e., where the cell voltage shows a steep gradient (see Figs. S.3.3–S.3.5).

Taking these influencing factors into account, the reconstruction-based approach can reliably trim the battery cell’s full-range dataset for a desired initialization limit, such as a reduced begin-of-discharge voltage
U
cell , init
dis
in the cases of voltage range restriction by
U
cell , cut
chg
, and approximate the battery cell’s energy-power characteristics with sufficient accuracy and effort, while accounting for the effects of electrical polarization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ERP utilization with a restricted operating range
ce:section.ce:para:
After validating the reconstruction-based approach for discharge initialization, reflected by the upper voltage limit reduction, extend its application by combining it with an additional variation in the battery cell’s discharge termination limits to describe its performance characteristics within a restricted operation range. Since the adjustment of these termination limits, such as the lower voltage limit
U
cell , min
or the upper current limit
I
cell , max
dis
, results in earlier termination of the respective CP regimes, performance characteristics can be trimmed directly from the dataset without reconstruction, as previously presented in [23]. The Ragone envelope is then defined by the dominant termination limit at each set power and thus represents the maximum energy loss Δ
E
cell , unav
dis
in relation to the base case. For this discussion, however, the focus will be exclusively on the voltage operating range here.

Using cell #1, Fig. 9 (a) illustrates the cell voltages
U

of the base case measurement from Fig. 2(a1), with the full range plotted in light gray and the restricted range in black. As an example, the minimum voltage limit

(green) is increased from the manufacturer’s limit of 1.5 – 2.0
V

(red) is reduced from the manufacturer’s limit of 2.7 – 2.5
V
using the reconstruction-based approach from Section 4. Considering the effect of electrical polarization, this reduced initialization limit trims the discharge curves along an isoline of the corresponding
SoC

rather than horizontally. As shown in the polarization plot of Fig. 5, the initial discharge voltage

Fig. 9(b) displays the associated ERP of cell #1 under the imposed operating range restrictions defined by

and

. The Ragone curve of its base case measurement from Fig. 3(a) is shown in light gray with consistent color coding. A separate increase in

results in the green Ragone envelope, where the energy-power characteristics of the battery cell are more limited in the upper part of this envelope up to the knee point, while the right flank remains limited by

, as in the base case. The vertical gap between the two Ragone curves describes the energy deficit due to earlier termination, referred to as Δ

.

Conversely, reducting

using the reconstruction-based approach via
SoC

yields the red Ragone envelope. Owing to the quasi-equidistant SoC isolines in the polarization plot, the shape of this Ragone curve closely resembles a vertical shift of the full-range envelope. The energy not available due to the later initialization is denoted as Δ
E

.

By superimposing the individual Ragone curves for the upper and lower limits, these non-available energy portions Δ
E
cell , cut
dis
are summed across the ERP at each set power. This graphical superposition is illustrated in Fig. 9(b) using the reference power
i
P
= 9 . The resulting Ragone envelope in black represents the battery cell’s performance characteristics within a restricted voltage range based on these exemplary limits. Subject to the limitations and recommendations outlined in Section 4.3, this ERP superposition can be applied to any combination of upper and lower limits, provided that a full-range dataset with sufficient resolution and accuracy is available for the given cell, allowing for both initialization and termination limits to be adjusted accordingly.

In this study, we have experimentally characterized the power-based performance of three commercial lithium-ion battery cells within a restricted operating range, primarily focusing on reducing the upper voltage limit. Five different cut-off voltages were applied to each battery cell to evaluate their influence on the subsequent discharge performance in terms of energy and power. The results revealed energy reductions that varied with cell type, with the specific cell chemistry playing a dominant role. Restricting the upper cell voltage can benefit system design or operational management by avoiding harmful voltage ranges without significantly compromising performance, especially for battery cells with a pronounced initial voltage drop. Based on our analysis, this applies to LTO- and LFP-based cells #1 and #3, while a linear decrease in energy must be considered when using the LCO-based cell #2 in practical applications where its end-of-charge voltage is reduced.

For evaluating these altered performance characteristics, the extended Ragone plot has proven to be an efficient concept. We introduced a reconstruction-based approach to enable recalculation of the desired discharge performance characteristics from a single base case dataset across the entire operating range. This approach matches the cut-off SoC from the preceding charge process, referenced by a pseudo-OCV measurement, to trim the dataset to a reduced begin-of-discharge voltage, allowing for the reconstruction of the polarization-adjusted initialization conditions. Although the reconstruction is effective, it is sensitive to potential uncertainties in the SoC matching procedure, especially for battery cells with a flat voltage plateau, such as cell #3, and pronounced electrical polarization behavior, which correspondingly accounts for severe temperature rise during operation, such as cell #2. Systematic deviations based on this temperature effect and an accurate selection of the pseudo-OCV were investigated and identified as methodological limitations for this approach. As a result, reliable utilization is recommended only for moderate reductions in the upper voltage limit, especially in parts of the operating voltage curve where the cell voltage exhibits a steep gradient, and both causes for deviations are largely negligible. This underlines the importance of considering the electrical polarization effect, which distinguishes the battery cell’s voltage-based operating range specification from often synonymously used descriptors such as SoC, SoE, or DoD.

Finally, we examined the superposition of upper and lower operating limits in the ERP using the reconstruction-based approach to extend its applicability to broader operating range restrictions under the limiting aspects of the previous validation. Considering these limitations, this approach enables comprehensive battery performance analysis without costly, additional cell characterization measurements at various combinations of upper and lower operating limits. Based on a global full-range CP-based dataset and a suitable pseudo-OCV reference, the reconstruction method provides a practical solution for battery selection and design with sufficient accuracy and minimal effort, accounting for the effects of electrical polarization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Further aspects of research and development
ce:section.ce:para: In order to harness these findings, continuous work will focus on integrating the reconstruction-based approach for handling initialization limits as an additional feature in our system design method. This enhancement would enable the ERP to support the development of adaptive operational management strategies, such as derating or multi-use storage applications. One potential application is the integration of a data-reduced ERP model into a battery management system, to evaluate its feasibility for rapid monitoring and real-time control of the battery’s performance by dynamically assessing its power and energy capabilities in variable operating ranges. Furthermore, extending performance characterizations to the charge direction would be beneficial, especially for fast charge strategies. In cases of restricted range cyclization, prioritizing the limiting direction, either charge or discharge, becomes crucial. Consequently, future work should explore mapping a combination of both within the ERP and taking additional thermal and degradation aspects into account.

However, it is important to acknowledge the inherent uncertainties in the reconstruction method, which depend on the quality of the underlying data and cell chemistry. As demonstrated across all three cells, the available cell energy was systematically overestimated by up to 3
% compared to the measurements, e.g., due to the temperature effect that was analyzed in detail but could not be accounted for the reconstruction yet. Given these intrinsic uncertainties in the practically relevant range, we recommend incorporating a generalized safety margin of at least 5
% of the respective cell energy when applying the reconstruction-based approach to adjust the upper voltage limit of any battery cell. To further improve accuracy, a refinement of the test setup incorporating more precise thermal analysis is advised. Specifically, investigating the battery cell’s performance characteristics at (i) different initial temperatures and (ii) cooling conditions using an ERP would be valuable, as both aspects are crucial for assessing real-world operational behavior of the battery.

Sven Wiegelmann: Writing – review & editing, writing – original draft, visualization, validation, software, resources, methodology, investigation, formal analysis, data curation, and conceptualization. Astrid Bensmann: Writing – review & editing, supervision, project administration, funding acquisition, and conceptualization. Richard Hanke-Rauschenbach: Writing – review & editing, supervision, resources, and conceptualization.
